result:
Dawn unfolds across the city like a slow chord struck on some cosmic piano. Rooftops glisten with leftover rain, each droplet a tiny mirror holding the whole violet sky. I walk barefoot across my apartment’s cold floor, the sole morning creature awake except for a cautious pigeon pacing the balcony rail. For a moment neither of us moves; we share that unspoken truce all living things negotiate when the world is still half dream, half substance.

In the kitchen I spill coffee grounds across the counter in the shape of an archipelago. Islands of dark earth, sea of Formica. I pretend each mound has its own weather: mist over Sumatra, typhoon off Java, a temperate drizzle in Costa Rica. Somewhere in those beans an entire history of soil and sunlight waits to be translated into caffeine and steam. I like that—how the planet travels farther in my mug than I probably will today.

The pigeon coos once, approval or warning, then launches, wings beating like two wet maples trying to applaud. It disappears into the bright slot between high-rises, and I imagine letters tied to its ankles: tiny strips confessing everything I still don’t know how to tell anyone aloud. I watch until the sky swallows even the silhouette, then sip the black ocean. For one fleeting minute I taste topography: the steep bitterness of mountain slopes, the mellow sweetness of lowland sun, and the faint iron note of the train tracks my grandfather laid decades ago in another continent.

Dawn unfolds across the city like a slow chord struck on some cosmic piano. Rooftops glisten with leftover rain, each droplet a tiny mirror holding the whole violet sky. I walk barefoot across my apartment’s cold floor, the sole morning creature awake except for a cautious pigeon pacing the balcony rail. For a moment neither of us moves; we share that unspoken truce all living things negotiate when the world is still half dream, half substance.

In the kitchen I spill coffee grounds across the counter in the shape of an archipelago. Islands of dark earth, sea of Formica. I pretend each mound has its own weather: mist over Sumatra, typhoon off Java, a temperate drizzle in Costa Rica. Somewhere in those beans an entire history of soil and sunlight waits to be translated into caffeine and steam. I like that—how the planet travels farther in my mug than I probably will today.

The pigeon coos once, approval or warning, then launches, wings beating like two wet maples trying to applaud. It disappears into the bright slot between high-rises, and I imagine letters tied to its ankles: tiny strips confessing everything I still don’t know how to tell anyone aloud. I watch until the sky swallows even the silhouette, then sip the black ocean. For one fleeting minute I taste topography: the steep bitterness of mountain slopes, the mellow sweetness of lowland sun, and the faint iron note of the train tracks my grandfather laid decades ago in another continent.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
